items.front: What must be issued when clearing an aircraft to a holding fix other than the destination?
items.back: Issue any route beyond the limit, holding instructions unless no delay is expected, and an EFC when delay is expected, taking operational factors into account.

items.front: When no delay is expected, what must a clearance beyond the holding fix include?
items.back: Issue it as soon as possible and include the new clearance limit or approach clearance, the route or "via last routing cleared," and any altitude assignment different from the current altitude.

items.front: What must ATC do when arrival delays reach or are expected to reach 30 minutes?
items.back: Advise the supervisor or flow controller early, and have the responsible center or terminal facility issue total delay information as soon as possible unless ATIS makes a pilot request unnecessary.

items.front: What elements belong in holding instructions?
items.back: Specify holding direction from the fix, the fix, the radial or route, leg length if needed, turn direction when needed, and maximum holding airspeed when the paragraph requires it.

items.front: When may a visual holding point be used?
items.back: You may use a surface reference point as the holding fix if the pilot is familiar with it and can identify it visually.

items.front: When may a pilot deviate from the prescribed holding flight path?
items.back: Approve the deviation if traffic and obstacle conditions permit.

items.front: What separation concern applies at an unmonitored NAVAID holding fix?
items.back: Protect the course the holding aircraft would follow if it stopped receiving signals from the NAVAID.

items.front: When must ATC keep aircraft from holding inbound near the ILS localizer?
items.back: When weather is below 800-foot ceiling or 2 miles visibility, do not authorize holding below 5,000 feet AGL inbound on or within 1 mile of the localizer between the outer marker or substitute fix and the airport.
